old_claim.note: Basic Rope Making is an earlier historical predecessor or foundation, not a one-to-one engineering dependency.

new_claim.note: Athenian inventories treated hypozomata hull-bracing ropes as the foremost hanging gear and a trireme as commissioned when fitted, making heavy cordage operationally required.

source_shape.source_locator: Abstract: Piraeus naval inventories list hypozomata first; a decree specifies fitting crews, and a trireme fitted with the four regulation ropes was regarded as in commission.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The study's inventory and decree evidence makes heavy hull-bracing ropes standard commissioned equipment of the Athenian trireme.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Cordage therefore did structural and operational work on the vessel rather than serving only as a remote Paleolithic precursor to later rigging.

ontology_before: Rope making was modeled only as ancient background and cited through unrelated Neanderthal fiber evidence.
ontology_after: Heavy cordage is a required direct prerequisite because hypozomata braced the hull and marked a trireme as commissioned.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed and locked by graph invariant 2026-07-16-greek-trireme-boundary: basic_rope_making remains required.
would_reject_if: Athenian naval records or a superior reconstruction show operational triremes commissioned without hypozomata or equivalent hull-bracing cordage.
short_reason: Piraeus inventories and a commissioning decree make hypozomata structural equipment, not merely historical background.
